awards.title: Collaborative Research: EPIIC Combining Partnerships and Entrepreneurship for Emerging Technologies (ComPEET)

papers.title: Why don’t democracies fight each other? The role of territorial issues
papers.abstract: Why don't democracies fight each other? Since discovering this empirical regularity, scholars have assumed that the answer must lie with regime type (i.e. democracy). Our paper provides and tests an alternative explanation: the territorial explanation of war, which stresses grievances and argues that territorial issues incentivize states to resort to war more often than disagreements over other, non-territorial issues. We show that democracies do not generally have territorial militarized interstate disputes (MIDs) or the territorial claims that would produce territorial MIDs. Democracies are peaceful because they lack the most dangerous grievances in the international system.

papers.title: Contribution of science, technology, and innovation to post-conflict in Colombia
papers.abstract: More than fifty years of conflict in Colombia has left a country with many needs. This article analyzes how Science, Technology, and Innovation (STI) can contribute to improving communities affected by violence in a post-conflict stage. This paper begins with an introduction focused on the processes of the generation of violence and the peace agreement, following the role of the UN in the post-conflict stage. A conceptual analysis of the actors involved in Science, Technology, and Innovation activities is also presentedwith one analysis of the activities of STI that can contribute to the post-conflict, continuing with some financing strategies for the activities of STI and a brief description of the legal framework STI against the post-conflict. The paper concludes with some recommendations and opportunities in this new era for the country.

papers.title: THE ROLE OF CITTASLOW MOVEMENT ON PLACE AUTHENTICITY, ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT AND QUALITY OF LIFE
Anomalies of the Wilsonian (Monadic) Democratic Peace in the Nineteenth Century: What Can They Tell Us?
papers.abstract: The Wilsonian Monadic Peace argues that not only do joint democracies not fight each other, but they are inherently peaceful. They get involved in war primarily by being attacked and rarely initiate wars. The institutional explanation of this monadic democratic peace maintains that democracies have this pacific tendency because the people use the legislature to restrain the executive. This paper argues that the best way to assess the causal logic underlying the institutional explanation is by comparing specific cases where war is avoided and where it occurs to see if legislatures and the public restrain leaders. Three historical cases from the Nineteenth Century, which were uncovered as part of a larger project, are reviewed in detail: one that did not go to war and two that did. Cases are drawn from the most democratic states at the time—France, England, and the U.S. In each instance the casual process did not work the way it was expected. Instead, the legislature and the “public” were more prone to war hysteria than the executive. Each of these cases is regarded as anomalous for the democratic peace. The implications of these three anomalies are explored in detail.

papers.abstract: understood and a future is foreseen—their questions and interests, their methods and procedures may in a limited sense be authentically their own, but they are, at the same time, situated within the boundaries of their own epoch's way of understanding the world. Thus, the discourse of the mid-nineteenth to early twentieth centuries was specific to events which were occurring within the spatial and temporal view of its authors. So, just as scholars then were talking about issues for which they had a contextual basis, scholars today talk about issues which are specific to our time—issues which are not necessarily new, but which are of greater intellectual and theoretical importance than in the past. For example, one might argue that among the defining issues today are the concepts of economic interdependence, regimes, and political economy. Though, as Schmidt points out, these issues are not unique to the discourse of international relations, it is quite possible that a hundred or so years from now scholars will be talking about different issues which will coincide with the context of their time. Thus, it is difficult to take exception to Schmidt's approach, or forcefully argue against his conclusions. The history of international relations demonstrates that there have been many issues and themes which have come to the forefront at different times. It is this continual "evolution" of the field which gives rise to Schmidt's work. Not so many years ago a rhetorical history of the field of international relations might not have been given serious hearing. However, as a result of the post-positivist movement in the field, Schmidt and others find themselves in a pluralistic environment which accepts a variety of interpretations of the issues important for study in the field. And even though Schmidt contends that the "myths" of realism and idealism continue to dominate the field (p. 227), his conclusion is but the observation of someone viewing the field from his own time. The debates which rage in international relations, in whatever age, are but constituent elements of the larger issues of international politics. No discourse in any age will ever fully capture the variety and complexity of issues which nations, and the international system itself, have to face. Thus, we must learn from history in order to make sense of our present, so that we may be able to anticipate the future.
